result:
:::writing
The first thing the city forgot was the sound of dogs.

It didn’t happen all at once. Nothing ever does. At first there were still a few—thin, suspicious creatures that watched from alleys and vanished when approached. Then there were fewer. Then none. People noticed, but only in passing, like realizing you haven’t heard a certain song on the radio in a while. The city was good at continuing.

Marin noticed because silence has edges, and she lived her life by edges.

She repaired things for a living. Not the obvious things like broken screens or cracked pipes—those had manuals, diagrams, a kind of sanctioned path back to usefulness. Marin worked on objects that had slipped out of their intended purposes. A clock that ran backward except on Tuesdays. A door that opened into rooms that didn’t exist. A set of spoons that changed weight depending on who held them. People brought these to her with embarrassment, as if confessing a private superstition.

She accepted them without comment. She listened. Things always told you what they were trying to be.

Her shop sat on a narrow street that the city planners had forgotten to widen. It was perpetually caught between shadow and light, as though the sun itself couldn’t decide what to do with it. The sign above the door read: REPAIRS, though someone had scratched a small question mark beside it long ago.

On the day the dogs were officially gone—when the last reported sighting was printed in a small column on page six—Marin was working on a radio that only played voices from rooms you had just left. It whispered apologies, half-finished jokes, and the ghost of a laugh that made you turn your head before remembering there was no one there.

“Stay,” she told it, gently adjusting a coil.

The radio crackled. For a moment, it held.

Then a voice said, very clearly, “You forgot something,” and the dial spun on its own.

Marin sat back. “Did I?” she asked.

The shop, as usual, did not answer.

Later, a man came in carrying a box lined with cloth. He held it with the careful posture of someone transporting a fragile truth.

“It’s not working,” he said.

Marin waited.

He opened the box. Inside was a collar. Leather, worn soft at the edges. A metal tag hung from it, engraved with a name: Harlow.

“It used to—” The man stopped. Swallowed. “It used to jingle. Even when he was still. Especially then. You could always find him.”

Marin picked up the collar. It was warm, though the day was cool.

“And now?” she asked.

“Now it doesn’t make any sound at all,” he said. “I shake it. I hang it. I—” He cut himself off. “I just thought maybe something inside it broke.”

Marin turned the collar over in her hands. There was no visible mechanism. No hollow chamber, no bead or bell. Just leather, metal, and the faint imprint of use.

“What was he like?” she asked.

The man blinked. “Excuse me?”

“Harlow.”

“Oh.” He looked down at the collar as if it might answer for him. “He was… loud. Not barking, exactly. Just—present. You always knew where he was. Even when you didn’t see him. Especially then.”

Marin nodded. “Leave it with me.”

After he left, she closed the shop early. The light outside had shifted into that indecisive gray that made the street feel like a held breath. She placed the collar on her workbench and sat across from it.

“Alright,” she said. “What are you trying to be?”

The collar did not respond. But something in the room tilted, just slightly, like a painting not quite level.

Marin reached out and lifted the collar again. This time, she didn’t look at it. She closed her eyes.

Edges.

She listened for the boundary between what was and what insisted on being.

At first, there was nothing. Then—faint, like a memory of a memory—a sound. Not a jingle. Not quite. More like the idea of a jingle, the space where one would fit. It flickered at the edge of hearing.

Marin followed it.

The shop dissolved around her in the way familiar places sometimes do when you stop paying attention to them. The workbench softened, the walls thinned, and the air took on that peculiar density of somewhere almost-real.

She was standing in a park she didn’t recognize.

The grass was too evenly green. The trees too evenly spaced. It looked like someone had tried to recreate a memory of a park without ever having sat in one.

And there, in the middle of it, was a shape.

It wasn’t a dog. Not exactly. It had the outline of one, the suggestion of movement, but it flickered, as if uncertain about its own form. When it turned toward her, its face was both there and not there, features shifting like reflections in disturbed water.

“Harlow?” Marin said.

The shape stilled.

For a moment, everything sharpened. The grass gained texture. The trees leaned in, as if listening.

The shape took a step toward her. There was no sound.

“That’s the problem, isn’t it,” Marin murmured.

She crouched, holding the collar out in front of her. “You’re still here,” she said. “But the city forgot how to hear you.”

The shape hesitated. Then, slowly, it approached.

Marin could feel the edge of it now—the boundary where presence met absence. It was thin. Too thin.

“Things don’t just stop,” she said, more to herself than to it. “They get… misplaced.”

She glanced around the park. It was too perfect. Too complete. Like a placeholder.

“A substitute,” she said. “For when the real thing is inconvenient.”

The shape flickered more intensely, as if agitated.

“I know,” Marin said. “I don’t like it either.”

She looked back at the collar in her hands. “You used to make a sound so people could find you. But if no one remembers to listen…”

She trailed off.

The idea came not as a solution but as a shift—a small, stubborn refusal to accept the given shape of things.

“Then we’ll make them remember,” she said.

Marin stood. The park wavered.

“How loud were you?” she asked.

The shape didn’t answer. But it stepped closer, and for a brief, impossible second, it was solid. Weight. Warmth. A presence that pushed against the air.

“Good,” Marin said.

She took the collar and, with careful hands, did nothing to it at all.

Instead, she changed the space around it.

Edges.

She reached for the boundary between sound and silence, the thin line the city had allowed to blur. She pulled it taut, like string drawn between two points. She anchored it to the collar, to the idea of Harlow, to the memory of presence that refused to be erased.

The park began to unravel.

The too-green grass dulled. The trees lost their symmetry. The sky cracked along invisible seams.

“Go on,” Marin said softly.

The shape moved.

This time, when it stepped, there was a sound.

Not a jingle. Not yet. But something closer. A suggestion becoming insistence.

The world snapped back.

Marin was in her shop, the collar in her hands. The radio on the bench hissed, then fell silent.

And from the collar—

A faint, unmistakable chime.

Marin smiled, just a little.

Outside, on the narrow street caught between shadow and light, someone paused.

“Did you hear that?” a voice said.

For the first time in a long while, the city hesitated.

It was a small thing. A nearly-nothing. But it had edges.

And edges, Marin knew, were where everything began.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
